steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The underlined claim states that 'Aha Pūnana Leo increased the number of young people who speak Hawaiian. To support it, we need direct evidence of growth in young Hawaiian speakers that is specifically tied to that school.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Besides Native Hawaiians, tens of thousands of people from other Pacific Islander communities live in Hawai'i today, including over 37,000 Samoans.: Describes Samoan residents, not Hawaiian-speaking youth. No connection to the school or language growth. ✗
  • Choice B — Fewer than fifty children could speak Hawaiian when 'Aha Pūnana Leo was founded, but now more than 2,000 students at 'Aha Pūnana Leo speak it.: Directly shows the before-and-after count of young Hawaiian speakers at the school, proving the claim of increase. ✓
  • Choice C — Hawaiian is very similar to other languages that are spoken on the Polynesian Islands of the Pacific Ocean, including Tahitian, Samoan, and Maori.: Discusses linguistic relationships, not speaker numbers or the school's impact. ✗
  • Choice D — Roughly 680,000 Native Hawaiian people lived in the United States in 2020, and a little less than half of them lived in Hawai'i.: A population statistic with no information about language ability or young speakers. ✗
B is correct.
